sections.body: Deciding in the moment, in the middle of temptation, is the worst time. Decide in advance, when you're calm: 'if this situation comes up, I'll do that.' These pre-set plans — called implementation intentions — work without requiring deliberation in the instant. You're not facing temptation empty-handed; you're following a plan you already made.

steps: Schedule hard tasks when your energy is high.
Turn intentions into fixed-time habits.
Prepare 'if... then...' plans in advance.
Reduce the number of unnecessary decisions in your day.
Rest: willpower recharges with sleep.

faq.q: Why do I always cave in the evenings?
faq.a: Your willpower reserve is lower by end of day — that's normal. The fix: automate as much as possible and avoid making important decisions at night.

title: Managing Automatic Negative Thoughts
intro: The brain constantly produces thoughts — many of them negative and inaccurate. You can learn not to believe all of them.

sections.heading: A thought isn't a fact
sections.body: Your brain generates thousands of thoughts per day, automatically. Many are negative, exaggerated, or simply false. The common mistake is treating them all as truth. The essential shift: a thought is a mental event, not a fact. You can observe it without having to buy into it.

sections.heading: Spot the distortions
sections.body: Negative thoughts often follow familiar twisted patterns: all-or-nothing thinking, catastrophizing, overgeneralization ('always,' 'never'), mind-reading. Learning to name the distortion ('there I go catastrophizing again') creates distance. You're no longer fighting the thought head-on — you're filing it as a known mental bug.

sections.heading: Challenge the thought
sections.body: When a negative thought digs in, play investigator: what evidence supports it, what evidence contradicts it? What would I say to a friend with this thought? What would a more accurate version sound like? The goal isn't forced positivity — it's replacing a false thought with one that's true and balanced.

steps: Remind yourself: a thought isn't a fact.
Name the distortion (catastrophizing, overgeneralizing...).
Find evidence for and against the thought.
Reframe it into a balanced, accurate version.
Return to one concrete action in the present.

faq.q: Can you eliminate negative thoughts?
faq.a: No — they're part of how the brain works. The goal isn't to eliminate them but to stop believing all of them and letting them make your decisions.

faq.q: What if negative thoughts are overwhelming?
faq.a: If they become constant or very painful, a therapist can teach you targeted tools. CBT is highly effective for this.

title: Trusting Your Own Decisions
intro: Endlessly second-guessing your choices is exhausting. You can learn to decide with more peace of mind.

sections.heading: The myth of the perfect choice
sections.body: Much decision anxiety comes from searching for THE right choice, as if there were one obvious correct answer. Reality: most choices have upsides and downsides, and several options can each be good. Dropping the idea of the perfect choice removes enormous pressure and makes deciding actually possible.

sections.heading: Decide with the information you have
sections.body: We often judge a past decision using information we didn't have at the time. That's unfair. A good choice is judged by what you knew at that moment, not in hindsight. Gather a reasonable amount of information, then decide. Waiting for total certainty means waiting indefinitely.

sections.heading: Commit rather than second-guess
sections.body: Once a decision is made, energy should go into making it work — not rehashing it. Many choices become good ones precisely because you invest in them fully. Telling yourself 'I decided, now I move forward and adjust if needed' transforms paralyzing doubt into active, settled commitment.

steps: Drop the idea that there's one perfect choice.
Gather reasonable information — don't wait for certainty.
Set yourself a deadline to decide.
Once decided, commit fully to making it work.
Judge your past choices by what you knew then.

faq.q: How do you handle a really important choice?
faq.a: Take more time and more information, write out pros and cons, talk it through. But still set a deadline — endless doubt never helps.

title: Freeing Yourself from Body Comparison
intro: Comparing your body to the ones you see everywhere chips away at self-esteem. You can build a more peaceful relationship with yours.

sections.heading: Unrealistic images everywhere
sections.body: Bodies shown in media and on social media are often selected, lit, posed, retouched, and sometimes digitally altered. Comparing your real body — seen from every angle, in real light — to these crafted images is a rigged comparison from the start. Knowing this doesn't erase everything, but it strips power from the feeling of inadequacy.

sections.heading: From body-as-object to body-as-ally
sections.body: We're taught to judge our bodies like objects to be evaluated. Yet your body is above all what allows you to live: to walk, laugh, create, feel. Shifting attention from 'what it looks like' to 'what it lets me do' changes the whole relationship. The body becomes an ally rather than a display to grade.

sections.heading: Care over punishment
sections.body: Taking care of your body out of kindness — eating well, moving, sleeping because it feels good — is sustainable. Punishing it out of self-rejection isn't, and it causes suffering. If you want to change something, do it from a place of respect, not hatred. Respect motivates better and longer than shame ever will.

steps: Remind yourself that the images you see are edited and curated.
Limit accounts that make you hate your body.
Write down one thing your body lets you do each day.
Talk about your body the way you'd talk about a friend.
Take care of yourself out of kindness, not punishment.

faq.q: What if I genuinely don't like my body?
faq.a: That's common and it's something that can be worked on. If the discomfort is intense or affects your eating, talk to a doctor or professional. You deserve support.

title: Staying Calm Under Pressure
intro: High-stakes situations trigger stress. A few key reflexes help you stay clear-headed when it counts.

sections.heading: Catch the early signs
sections.body: You don't lose your calm all at once — your body warns you first (short breath, tension, voice rising, thoughts racing). Learning to spot these signals early is crucial. The earlier you detect the surge, the easier it is to intervene. Once you're at the peak, regaining control is much harder.

sections.heading: Ground yourself in the body
sections.body: Under pressure, the mind panics — but the body can serve as an anchor. Plant your feet firmly, feel the ground, extend your exhale. Consciously relax your shoulders and jaw. These simple moves send the brain a safety signal. Coming back to the body breaks the catastrophic thought spiral and restores clarity.

sections.heading: Slow down before acting
sections.body: Pressure pushes you to react fast — usually badly. The winning reflex is to insert a micro-delay: one breath, two seconds, before responding or deciding. That tiny space gives the rational brain time to come back online. You respond from calm, not from raw reaction.

steps: Learn to recognize your early stress signals.
Plant your feet on the ground and extend your exhale.
Consciously relax your shoulders and jaw.
Insert one breath before responding or deciding.
Practice these reflexes on small everyday stressors first.

faq.q: How do you train for high-stakes situations when they're rare?
faq.a: Practice the reflexes — breathing, grounding — on small daily stresses. Well-rehearsed, they'll be available on the day something real is on the line.
